Article.ArticleTitle: Fibrous cardiac patches incorporating mxene and decellularized pericardium enhance myocardial repair in infarcted rats.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study developed and evaluated a multifunctional electrospun cardiac patch integrating decellularized pericardium (DP) and MXene nanosheets within a poly (lactic acid) (PLA) matrix for myocardial infarction repair. Calf pericardium was effectively decellularized using a gentle 1 M NaCl protocol, preserving essential extracellular matrix (ECM) components (GAGs, collagen) while eliminating cellular material. Ti₃C₂Tₓ MXene nanosheets were successfully synthesized from Ti₃AlC₂ MAX phase via HF etching, confirmed by FESEM, EDS, XRD, and FTIR analyses. Electrospun scaffolds were optimized for composition and structure. The PLA70:DP30 formulation exhibited uniform nanofibers (398 ± 38 nm diameter) and favorable mechanical properties. Incorporating MXene significantly enhanced electroconductivity (reaching ~2 μA/V with 8 % MXene), hydrophilicity (contact angle reduced to 33°), mechanical strength (Young's modulus 55.27 ± 5.07 MPa), swelling, and controlled degradation. Scaffolds containing ≥4 % MXene demonstrated potent antibacterial activity (>90 % inhibition of S. aureus and E. coli after 24 h). In vitro, PLA70:DP30 scaffolds with 4-8 % MXene significantly improved H9c2 cardiomyoblast adhesion, spreading, and viability compared to pure PLA. Crucially, in vivo transplantation onto infarcted rat hearts revealed that the PLA70:DP30 patch with 8 % MXene yielded superior therapeutic outcomes. It significantly improved cardiac function (ejection fraction, fractional shortening), reduced left ventricular dilation and scarring (smaller ischemic area: 21 % vs. 34.62 % in MI controls), increased ventricular wall thickness, enhanced cardiomyogenesis (elevated cTnT expression), and promoted robust neovascularization within the infarct zone. These results demonstrate the efficacy of this biomimetic, conductive, and bioactive patch for promoting cardiac repair post-myocardial infarction.
